title: 💡 Smart Locks: Security Risks They Don't Tell You
description:
Smart locks promise convenience and modern security. The reality includes risks most marketing skips. In this video I break down the documented security flaws in major smart locks, the lock pickers' findings from DEF CON conferences, and the practical setup choices that make smart locks safer than traditional locks.

Smart home, smart locks, home security, IoT vulnerabilities, August lock, Schlage, Yale, smart home 2026.

segments.voice: Smart locks promise security, convenience, and modern features. They also have unique attack vectors that traditional locks don't. According to Consumer Reports' twenty twenty-five smart lock testing, every major brand had at least one documented security concern. Some are minor configuration choices. Others are real flaws that allow remote open under specific conditions. Knowing them helps you set up safely.
segments.visual: smart lock door installation security camera

segments.voice: Let me start with what makes smart locks different from traditional. A traditional pin tumbler lock can be picked, bumped, or kicked in. A smart lock adds wireless attack surface, app vulnerabilities, cloud account compromise, and battery failure. The threat model expands. According to DEF CON Lock Bypass research from twenty twenty-three through twenty twenty-five, most smart locks remain physically secure but add new digital risks.
segments.visual: traditional versus smart lock attack surface

segments.voice: The most common real-world attack on smart locks. Account compromise. If your smart lock account email is compromised through a phishing attack, the attacker can often reset the lock or grant themselves access without ever touching the door. According to a twenty twenty-four FBI report on cybercrime, account-based smart device intrusions tripled from twenty twenty-one to twenty twenty-four. Use a unique strong password and two-factor authentication. Always.
segments.visual: phishing email account compromise warning

segments.voice: Two-factor authentication on the lock account is critical. Most major brands now support it. August, Schlage, Yale, Kwikset, all have two-factor in their apps. Enable it during initial setup. The thirty seconds of inconvenience prevents the most common attack pattern. According to the Identity Theft Resource Center's twenty twenty-five report, accounts with two-factor were ninety-nine percent less likely to be compromised in mass breaches.
segments.visual: two factor authentication phone code SMS

segments.voice: Now let's talk about wireless protocols. Smart locks come in several flavors. Wi-Fi locks like the Schlage Encode and Yale Assure with Connect connect directly to your router. Z-Wave and Zigbee locks like the Schlage Connect and Yale Assure SL connect to a hub. Bluetooth locks like the original August connect only to your phone. Each protocol has different security trade-offs.
segments.visual: wireless protocols Wi-Fi Bluetooth Zigbee Z-Wave

segments.voice: Z-Wave is generally considered the most secure protocol for smart locks. The Z-Wave Security S2 framework provides encryption and authentication that has been audited extensively. According to a twenty twenty-three Silicon Labs security audit, Z-Wave Plus with S2 had no known critical vulnerabilities affecting smart locks. The downside is Z-Wave locks need a hub, which adds complexity.
segments.visual: Z-Wave S2 encryption security badge

segments.voice: Wi-Fi locks have a more varied security record. The convenience of direct internet access also means a wider attack surface. The August Wi-Fi Smart Lock had a vulnerability disclosed in twenty twenty-two that allowed packet sniffing on poorly secured networks. The fix was a firmware update, but unpatched locks remained vulnerable for months. Always keep firmware current.
segments.visual: Wi-Fi lock firmware update warning vulnerable

segments.voice: Bluetooth locks have the most documented attack research. DEF CON twenty twenty-three featured at least four presentations on Bluetooth Low Energy vulnerabilities in smart locks. The attacks generally require proximity to the lock, around thirty feet maximum. Real-world risk is limited but not zero. The August second generation and below had specific known vulnerabilities. Newer hardware fixed most.
segments.visual: Bluetooth attack research conference proximity

segments.voice: Let's discuss specific brands. The August Smart Lock series has been in market since twenty fourteen. The newest fourth generation has improved security significantly over earlier models. It still has a Bluetooth vulnerability disclosed in twenty twenty-three that requires firmware patching. Owners with older models should upgrade or maintain firmware religiously. Solid value at one hundred fifty dollars.
segments.visual: August fourth generation smart lock

segments.voice: The Schlage Encode and Encode Plus are widely considered among the most secure consumer smart locks. The Encode runs Wi-Fi directly. The Encode Plus adds Apple Home Key and Matter support. Both have ANSI Grade One certification, the highest residential rating, and have not had a critical security vulnerability disclosed since launch. Around two hundred fifty to three hundred dollars depending on style.
segments.visual: Schlage Encode Plus ANSI Grade One certified

segments.voice: The Yale Assure series, especially the Assure Lock two, is competitive with Schlage on security. Yale's parent company is Assa Abloy, the world's largest lock manufacturer, and the engineering reflects that pedigree. The Assure Lock two with the Wi-Fi module is around two hundred sixty dollars. Z-Wave version is around two hundred. Both are excellent choices.
segments.visual: Yale Assure Lock two installation

segments.voice: The Aqara U200 deserves attention as a budget option that takes security seriously. Around one hundred eighty dollars, supports Apple Home Key, Matter, and traditional keys. The build quality is good for the price. The privacy track record is solid because Aqara's local-first philosophy minimizes cloud exposure. Newer brand, less long-term track record, but positive early signals.
segments.visual: Aqara U200 budget smart lock features

segments.voice: What I would avoid. Smart locks from unknown brands selling at heavily discounted prices. The cheap unbranded smart locks on Amazon for under one hundred dollars often have undocumented Chinese cloud backends, default passwords, or shared encryption keys across the entire product line. Spend the extra hundred dollars for a known brand. Your home security is not the place to economize.
segments.visual: warning cheap unbranded smart locks avoid

segments.voice: Here's the catch. Even high-quality smart locks have a fundamental physical concern. The deadbolt mechanism behind the smart electronics is a standard pin tumbler lock. If you replace your existing deadbolt with a smart lock that has a physical key override, that key is the weakest link. Pick a smart lock with high-quality cylinder, like Schlage's Primus or Medeco compatible options, if traditional lock picking is in your threat model.
segments.visual: physical key cylinder picking traditional lock

segments.voice: Some smart locks like the Level Lock and Lockly Visions have done away with the external traditional key entirely. The Level Lock fits inside an existing deadbolt and operates entirely electronically. No external keyhole means no traditional pick attack. The trade-off is no physical fallback if the electronics fail. Battery management becomes critical.
segments.visual: Level Lock no key hole electronic only

segments.voice: Battery failure is a real concern with smart locks. Most use four AA batteries that last six to twelve months depending on usage. Some lower power models last up to two years. Battery alerts at twenty percent give you weeks of warning. According to the LockedIn smart lock survey from twenty twenty-four, fifteen percent of users had been locked out at least once due to battery failure they ignored. Plan for it.
segments.visual: battery low warning smart lock LCD display

segments.voice: External battery jump options are worth understanding. Several smart locks have nine-volt battery contacts on the exterior keypad. If the internal batteries die and you don't have keys, you can press a nine-volt battery against the contacts to power the keypad temporarily. The Schlage Encode, August, and Yale have this feature. Read the manual when you install.
segments.visual: nine volt battery jumper exterior contacts

segments.voice: User code best practices. Don't use four-digit codes when six is available. Don't share your master code with house cleaners, dog walkers, or contractors. Create temporary codes with expiration dates. According to a twenty twenty-three University of Michigan home security study, smart lock users with shared master codes were five times more likely to experience unauthorized entry. Manage codes deliberately.
segments.visual: smart lock keypad codes management individual

segments.voice: Auto-lock settings. Configure auto-lock to sixty seconds maximum. Five minutes is too long. The whole point of a smart lock is consistent locking, not occasional locking. Auto-lock at thirty seconds means even if you forget, the door secures itself promptly. According to Consumer Reports' twenty twenty-four smart home security guide, configurable auto-lock is the single feature with the highest correlation to reduced break-ins.
segments.visual: auto lock timer setting smart lock app

segments.voice: Geofencing and auto-entry are the most controversial smart lock features. Automatic entry when you arrive home is convenient but creates risk if your phone is stolen or compromised. Geofencing accuracy can also fail. The August has had widely-reported issues with auto-entry firing prematurely. Personally, I disable auto-entry and use a six-digit code. Mild inconvenience, real security improvement.
segments.visual: geofencing GPS auto entry disabled feature

segments.voice: In my own setup, I run a Schlage Encode Plus with two-factor on the account, six-digit codes for family members, temporary codes for guests, sixty-second auto-lock, manual entry only, and remote view of access logs through Apple Home. The setup feels thoughtful, not paranoid. Five minutes of careful configuration gets you most of the security upgrade.
segments.visual: thoughtful setup configuration smart lock

segments.voice: Network security for smart locks. Put your smart lock on a separate IoT network if your router supports VLANs. The lock can talk to the internet for remote control but cannot reach your laptop or file server. If the lock gets compromised, the attacker is contained to the IoT network. Most modern routers including Eero, Asus, and Unifi support VLAN setup in five minutes.
segments.visual: router VLAN IoT network segregation diagram

segments.voice: Insurance implications. Some homeowners insurance offers discounts for verified smart locks, similar to leak detection. State Farm, Allstate, USAA, and Liberty Mutual all have programs. The discount is typically smaller than leak detection, around three to five percent on the security portion of your premium. Worth asking about, but not a primary justification.
segments.visual: insurance discount smart lock home security badge

segments.voice: When smart locks are not the right choice. Multi-unit buildings where you don't control the door frame. Older doors with non-standard deadbolt configurations. Houses with extreme cold climates that exceed lithium battery operating ranges. Locations with unreliable power and no Wi-Fi. Each scenario has specific concerns that may rule out smart locks. Sometimes a high-quality traditional deadbolt is the right answer.
segments.visual: scenarios smart locks not appropriate

segments.voice: Final security recommendations. Pick a known brand with security track record. Enable two-factor on the account. Use unique six-digit codes per family member. Configure short auto-lock. Disable auto-entry if you can live without it. Keep firmware current. Put the lock on a separate network if possible. Have a backup physical key stored securely off-site or with a trusted neighbor. Layered security still applies in the smart era.
segments.visual: checklist secure setup smart lock recommendations

segments.voice: Subscribe to EspacioInteligente and hit the bell. Next week's setup goes deeper into smart smoke detectors and how they compare to traditional ones across speed, accuracy, and false-alarm rates. Drop a comment with which brand of smart lock you're considering, and I will share specific configuration tips for your situation.
segments.visual: subscribe button notification bell youtube channel
